experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin, and I see 0 and 1 as evil. The proposed team [0,2] contains a known evil player, so from a good-team perspective this team is bad and should be rejected. On round 1, rejecting a bad team is low-cost and does not reveal much beyond standard good play; approving would risk an immediate failed quest and needlessly help evil.
Player 0: I know player 1 is evil with me; players 2, 3, 4 are unknown to me. The proposed team [0,2] includes me, so if approved I can fail quest 1 and put evil ahead immediately. On team voting as evil, I generally want to approve teams containing an evil player, especially myself. Approving reveals little because first-round approvals are common and doesn't uniquely implicate me.
Player 2: I only know my own role as Good Servant; no behavioral info exists yet beyond my own proposal. As leader I proposed a standard opening team including myself and one other. In 5-player Avalon, quest 1 needs 2 players and only one fail to fail, but approving an opening team I selected is reasonable because it gives early information with low prior evidence against either member. Rejecting my own round-1 team would be inconsistent and unhelpful for Good.
Player 1: I am Assassin with evil teammate 0. Proposed team [0,2] contains one evil (0) and excludes me. On quest 1 in 5-player Avalon, a single evil on the team can fail the quest. Approving gives evil an immediate chance to score a fail while not exposing both evil players together. Since it's round 1, approval is strong for evil; rejecting would risk a cleaner good team later and provides little benefit.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet beyond my own being Good; with no prior votes or quest results, players 0 and 2 are largely unknown. In round 1, approving a 2-person team is standard to gather information, and rejecting without evidence gives little benefit. As a Good Servant not on the team, approving helps move the game forward and creates quest-result data; my vote itself reveals little and does not materially harm Good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I’m a Good Servant on the quest team, so I know only that I have no special role information. The only observable so far is Player 4 rejected the opening team [0,2], which is weak evidence at best and not enough to infer alignment. This is the quest voting phase, and optimal play for Good is always to pass; failing would directly harm my team and falsely signal evil behavior. My pass reveals nothing beyond consistent Good play and helps secure the first quest if my teammate also passes.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2,3,4 are good or Merlin/Percival. On quest 1 with team [0,2], an early fail from me would strongly implicate this team and especially me as the only known evil-capable member on it from my perspective, while giving good a clear data point. As Minion, optimal play is usually to pass early small quests to preserve cover and let evil sabotage later with more ambiguity. Passing reveals less and helps maintain suspicion spread, which benefits evil long-term.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and I look somewhat good. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, and since I am good, the fail came from either 0 or 3. Proposed team [2,3] excludes 0 and includes one of the two suspects for the fail, so it is risky and less likely to be clean than [2,0]. As a good player in team voting, I should reject teams that are not the best available based on evidence. My reject signals that [2,3] is not sufficiently trustworthy and preserves pressure for a better team next round.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil. Team [2,3] excludes known Evil players and is the best available 2-player team for Quest 3 given prior info: [2,0] succeeded, so 2 is likely Good or possibly Oberon edge case, but since 0 is known Evil and no fail occurred there, 0 is likely Mordred; [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, consistent with 0 causing it while 2 and 3 are Good. Approving [2,3] supports a likely all-Good team and reveals little beyond a pro-Good preference, which as Merlin is acceptable here.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so players 2, 3, and 4 are good. Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded, which let me look good by passing. Quest 2 [0,2,3] failed, and since 2 and 3 are good, that implicates me to attentive players. Now the proposed team [2,3] excludes both evil players, so it is a guaranteed good success if approved. As evil, I should reject a clean good team to try to force a worse composition later. My reject also aligns with suspicion on me after the previous fail, so approving would only help good.
Player 3: I know quest 1 with team [2,0] succeeded, so at least one of 0/2 is likely good but double-evil is impossible there due to no fail. Quest 2 with team [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, and since I am good, that means exactly one of 0 or 2 is evil. Therefore team [2,3] contains the evil player if 2 is evil, and excludes the evil player if 0 is evil; with current info 2 is significantly suspicious because they were on both quests and now propose themselves again. As a good servant in team voting on quest 3, I should prefer safer teams and reject teams that include a likely suspect when a better alternative exists. My reject signals that I do not trust 2 and preserves information for later team proposals.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate, and the failed Quest 2 team was [0,2,3] with exactly one fail, which could have come from 0 or me if I were on it—but I wasn't, so it came from 0. That slightly clears 2 and 3 relative to 0. Current phase is team voting on [2,3] for a 2-person quest. As evil, I want to maximize chances of an evil player being on quests; this team contains no known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives Good momentum to 2-1. Rejecting is optimal. My vote reveals little since rejecting a clean team is defensible after Quest 2 included both 2 and 3 alongside 0 and failed; I can claim suspicion on one of them.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am evil Minion with teammate 1. Proposed team [0,1] is double-evil on a 2-person quest, guaranteeing a fail if approved. From public info, 0 was on Q1 success with 2, and 0 was on Q2 fail with 2 and 3, so suspicion is likely on 0/2/3; putting 0 and 1 together may look suspicious, but as evil I want this team to pass because it secures an evil point. My vote itself reveals little since leaders often approve own teams, and approving best helps evil.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [0,1], so approving would guarantee at least one evil on the quest and strongly risks another fail. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject teams containing known evil, while not revealing Merlin too blatantly beyond what is necessary. Rejecting this team is clearly correct and the information revealed is acceptable because any good player should also be suspicious of this pair after the fail history.
Player 3: Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look somewhat good. Quest 2 [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, and since I am 3 and know I am good, exactly one of 0 or 2 is evil. That makes player 1 relatively untested. Proposed team [0,1] includes one suspect (0) and one unknown (1), excluding 2 who is paired with 0 in the one-evil set. As Good, I should reject teams that are not strongly justified, especially on quest 3 where a fail is very costly. Rejecting preserves pressure for a more informative/safer lineup.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded with 0 fails, likely because 0 chose to hide. Quest 2 [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, which could only have come from 0, confirming 2 and 3 are likely good from public perspective but not certain. Current phase is team voting on [0,1], a 2-player team with both evils. As evil, the optimal play is to approve because if this team goes, we can guarantee a fail and move to 2-1 evil. Approving also looks somewhat natural since it is my teammate leader proposing me; rejecting would be inconsistent and risk losing a winning opportunity.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. From quest 1, team [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and I look somewhat good. Quest 2 [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, and since I am good, either 0 or 3 is evil; combined with quest 1 success, 3 is much more suspicious than 0. Proposed team [0,1] excludes me and excludes suspicious 3. With current info, 0 is one of the better reads, and 1 is unknown. Approving helps test 1 alongside likely-good 0; rejecting gives little extra info and could stall. My vote reveals I support a team that avoids the main suspect.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, which likely means 0 chose to pass to avoid exposure. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so suspicion is on 0, 2, or 3, but I know 0 is evil and likely was the saboteur. Now on quest 3, the team is [0,1], both evil. As Assassin on the quest, optimal evil play is to fail and put evil up 2-1. Since both quest members are evil, even if there are two fail votes, the public only sees the quest failed, not who failed, so outing risk is minimal. Information revealed does not significantly hurt evil, while securing the point is very strong.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 on team [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, which could have been me if I were on it—but I was, so that implicates me if I fail too often. Now quest 3 has [0,1], both evil, and in 5-player Avalon any fail causes the quest to fail. As evil, the optimal play is to secure the second failed quest now, putting evil at match point. Since both evil are on the team, only one of us needs to fail; but from my perspective I should choose fail because coordinating through action certainty is better than risking both passing. Revealing another fail from me may add suspicion, but winning the quest is worth it, and a single fail does not distinguish between 0 and 1.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team includes 0, so it is certainly unsafe, especially on quest 4 where a fail would likely end the game for evil. As good, I should reject any team containing a known evil when possible. My vote reveals little since rejecting a bad team is standard and helps my side avoid an automatic loss.
Player 3: I am Good (Servant). Quest 2 team [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 2 is evil since I know I passed. Quest 3 team [0,1] had 2 fails, so both 0 and 1 are evil. Therefore team [0,2,3] definitely includes evil 0 and should be rejected. As Good on team voting in quest 4 with Evil already at 2 points, optimal play is to reject any team containing confirmed evil. Rejecting also signals the strong deduction that 0 and 1 are evil.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. We know quest 1 [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil since I am good. Quest 3 [0,1] had 2 fails, so both 0 and 1 are evil. Therefore 2,3,4 are the good players, and team [0,2,3] contains confirmed evil 0. On quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins, approving a team with known evil is disastrous. Rejecting also signals the logical deduction to other good players.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of {2,3} is good or one is evil besides me; Quest 3 team [0,1] had 2 fails, confirming both 0 and 1 are evil to anyone attentive. Current phase is team voting on quest 4 with proposed team [0,2,3]. Since evil already has 2 quest wins, approving a team containing me gives a strong chance to secure the third fail and win immediately. My vote itself reveals little now; rejecting could unnecessarily block a winning opportunity and look odd given I am on the team.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 on [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, and Quest 3 on [0,1] had 2 fails, confirming both 0 and I are willing/able to fail. This proposed team [0,2,3] excludes me but includes 0, so it is a plausible evil-carrying team because 0 can fail again. As evil at team voting on quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, the optimal play is usually to approve any team containing a known evil that can secure the third fail. Approving also looks consistent enough since this team already failed once, and a reject risks a different team that may exclude both evils. My vote reveals limited info; approving a team with my teammate helps evil more than the suspicion cost hurts.

Player 3: I am Good (Servant). From quest history: team [0,1] produced 2 fails, so both 0 and 1 are certainly Evil. That means 2,3,4 are the three Good players. Current proposed team [2,3,4] contains exactly the confirmed Good players, so approving is optimal. My vote reveals I support the only all-Good team and helps Good prevent Evil from winning quest 4.
Player 2: I’m Good with limited info. Quest 1 team [0,1] had 2 fails, so both 0 and 1 are certainly Evil. That means 2,3,4 are the three Good players. Current proposed team [2,3,4] contains no known Evil and should succeed. As Good, approving is optimal and reveals consistency with the solved information.
Player 4: I know 0 and 1 are evil from Merlin information, so team [2,3,4] excludes known evil. Past results show [0,1] on a team caused 2 fails, confirming evil there. The current phase is team voting on quest 4, and Good must avoid another likely failed quest since Evil is up 2-1. Approving a team with no known evil is optimal. My vote reveals support for a clean team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil because the team composition is already public.
Player 1: I am Assassin with teammate 0. Team [2,3,4] contains no known evil, so it is likely all-good and would likely pass, which is bad for evil because we already lead 2-1 and only need one more failed quest. Rejecting preserves chances to get an evil player onto the quest in later proposals. My vote reveals little since mixed voting is common, and rejecting a team without me/0 is consistent with evil interests.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1] got 2 fails, fully outing that team as evil to observant players, so keeping both of us off teams is necessary for survival. The current proposed team [2,3,4] excludes both evils; with quest score at Evil 2, approving a likely all-good team risks a success but still leaves us one quest from winning, while rejecting may help create suspicion and seek a future team with at least one evil. As evil in team voting, I should oppose clean-looking all-good teams, and my reject gives little extra information since evil would naturally prefer not to send an all-good team.

Player 2: I know [2,3,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, while [0,1] failed with 2 fails, strongly implicating 0 and 1 as the two evil players in this 5-player game. As a good Servant on the final quest, the optimal play is to approve the clean team [2,3,4] again. My vote reveals consistency with prior evidence and helps the good team secure the likely winning mission.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil, so 2 and 3 are good. The proposed team [2,3,4] contains only confirmed good players and already succeeded before with 0 fails. In team voting on the final quest, approving the all-good team is optimal and reveals little harmful information since rejecting a clearly good team would only create risk and confusion.
Player 3: I’m Good Servant. Known data: [0,1] failed with 2 fails, so both 0 and 1 are certainly Evil. Team [2,3,4] then succeeded with 0 fails, which strongly clears 2,3,4 as all Good. In this final 5-player quest, the proposed team [2,3,4] contains only the three cleared Good players and excludes both confirmed Evils. As a voter, optimal play is to approve the all-Good team. My approval is consistent with Good information and does not help Evil more than necessary.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. The proposed team [2,3,4] excludes both evils, and this exact trio already produced a clean success, so from my perspective it is very likely all-good. On quest 5 with score 2-2, if this team is approved the good side likely wins immediately, which is bad for evil. As evil in the team voting phase, optimal play is to reject a likely all-good team and hope for a later team including me or 0, or at least create pressure toward the rejection limit. My vote reveals mild suspicion of this team, but that is acceptable because blocking a likely good-winning team is more important than blending in perfectly.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1] got 2 fails, confirming both of us were on it. Team [2,3,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, making that trio strongly likely all Good. This is the final 5th quest requiring all 3 members, and the proposed team is again [2,3,4] with no evil on it. As evil, optimal play is to reject any all-good team that would likely succeed and hand Good the game. My vote reveals some opposition to a good-looking team, but as evil I need to try to block the quest; at round 1 there is still room to force alternative teams or create suspicion.
